topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of aged paper and lavender always brought a tightening sensation to my chest. I sat across from him, Dr. Alistair Vance, his gaze steady and profoundly calm. Alistair, the architect of my awkward adolescence, now wore a tweed jacket that seemed woven from quiet authority. He spoke about transference, about the deep roots of early relational patterns, and I found myself shrinking into the worn armchair. Every anecdote he shared about navigating complex emotional landscapes felt like an accusation. I kept fiddling with the frayed edge of my sleeve, my knuckles white against the fabric. When he asked me to articulate the source of my deepest insecurities, the words caught in my throat, thick and unformed. I just stared at the polished wood of the coffee table, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.

[story 2]

It was the quiet hum of the office that unnerved me most. Alistair, who used to relish the sharp edge of confrontation, now navigated the delicate terrain of vulnerability with an unnerving gentleness. I watched him from the doorway, a spectator to a performance I no longer understood. He was listening intently to a young patient, his expression shifting from professional empathy to something almost… familiar. A dull ache settled low in my stomach, a persistent pressure that made my shoulders tense. I wanted to walk in, to interrupt the flow, to demand an explanation for the transformation. Instead, I just stood there, rooted to the spot, feeling a strange, heavy weight pressing down on my chest, a feeling akin to having made a terrible mistake I couldn't undo.

[story 3]

I found myself drawn back to the university library where Alistair held his seminars. He was discussing trauma recovery, his voice low and measured, weaving narratives of healing from broken foundations. Seeing him now, so composed, so utterly in control of the narratives he presented, brought a profound sense of misalignment within me. I remember the sharp, stinging words I’d hurled at him years ago, the sheer, unadulterated cruelty of my youth. Now, observing his measured responses to distress, a slow, corrosive tide began to rise within me. I traced the spine of a book with my finger, the movement slow and deliberate, as a deep-seated sense of wrongness settled over me, a heavy cloak I couldn't shake off.
